EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough associated with a very cold upper low brought areas of heavy snow to parts of the western Washington lowlands, including Western Whatcom County, Everett and Vicinity, Seattle/Bremerton Area, and East Puget Sound Lowlands. The pass was closed for about 6 hours.

Deep low pressure moved from the eastern Pacific northeast into northern Vancouver Island, creating strong pressure differences and strong damaging southerly winds across much of western Washington. The wind blew down trees and knocked power to nearly 240,000 customers at the height of the storm, including about 180,000 in the Puget Sound region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 20,000 residents lost power.

One to three inches of snow that fell in the Spokane area contributed to numerous traffic accidents.  One collision resulted in an unknown number of indirect injuries as a car slid off the road into a group of people standing outside another vehicle involved in a separate collision.  Most of the crashes and slide-offs occurred along Interstate 90.

A moist pacific system moved into the inland northwest with about an inch of snow falling on the Palouse on the 29th and early on the 30th. Wind gusts to near 45 MPH with the storm occurred throughout the day on the 30th, creating areas of blowing snow.  Winds created significant drifting of snow and whiteout conditions, which closed Highway 195 just south of Pullman to the top of Lewiston Hill.  State Route 272 from Colfax to the Idaho Border was also closed.  Winds at the Pullman Airport gusted to near 45 MPH.

A strong winter storm brought heavy snow accumulations to much of central and northern Washington. A cold arctic air mass that moved into the area at the end of November was replaced by a moist southwest flow coming up from Hawaii. This resulted in a prolonged period of snowfall as warm moist air overran cold air at the surface.  The East Slopes of the North Washington Cascades was hardest hit by the storm where one to two feet of accumulation was recorded, with a spotter 18 miles northwest of Entiat measuring 24 inches, with another spotter near Leavenworth picking up 20 inches.  The northwest part of Wenatchee was also hit hard with 3/8 of ice accumulation on top of four to seven inches of new snow accumulation from the storm.  Elsewhere...on the Waterville Plateau, Okanogan Valley, Okanogan Highlands, and NE Washington Mountains snow accumulations mostly ranged from four to nine inches, except for near a foot of new snow at local ski resorts in NE Washington.

A weather system dropping down from Canada clipped extreme eastern Washington resulting in heavy snow accumulations across portions of the palouse.  Colfax received the heaviest snow accumulation where five inches was measured.  Elsewhere, three inches was reported in Pullman and St. John, with one inch in La Crosse.  Highway 195 near Round Barn Hill outside Pullman was closed for about two hours after several semi-trucks stopped due to the snowy conditions.

A strong warm Pacific frontal system brought widespread steady rain to the forecast area over a period of 36 hours. This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range and Willapa Hills, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley including Clark County, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total accumulation of 2.13 inches was reported at Castle Rock.

This system brought between 2 to 4 inches of rain to the Coast Range and Willapa Hills, between 1 to 3 inches to the coast and Willamette Valley including Clark County, and between 2 to 5 inches to the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total accumulation of 1.71 inches was reported at Washougal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year brought a blast of cold air to the region along with and following a strong Pacific frontal system. This brought snow to every area in the county warning area, including heavy amounts of snow to areas along the coast. Schools all over the region were closed for at least one day, more in places were driving remained perilous. A minimum of 1 to 4 inches of snow fell over the entire area, with heavier amounts reported on the coast, in the Coast Range and Cascades, and in portions of the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches fell over the majority of the inland valley areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful pacific storm brought very strong winds to portions of southwest Washington.  Many locations along the coast saw wind gusts in excess of 80 mph, while areas inland saw less severe, however still damaging, winds.  Power outages were sustained in multiple communities across the area. At it's peak, approximately 17000 Washington customers were without power due to this storm. Many power outages were due to downed trees or tree limbs knocking down power lines.  This also caused multiple highways from the coast to the interior to be closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were reported at the Abernathy RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds brought damaging winds to the Oroville and Republic areas.  Wind gusts up to 80 mph were recorded in Oroville.  These winds pulled up a tree by its roots and damaged the roof of a home.  In Republic, 60-70 MPH winds occurred.  Republic lost power from 12:30 PM through 7:30 PM.  Numerous trees were blown down and a large branch fell on a car.  Property damage from this storm is estimated at $7,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a Pacific low pressure storm, cold air was entrenched over southwest Washington. As a result, post-frontal unstable shower activity resulted in additional snow accumulations over the lower elevations. Schools in the greater Portland and Vancouver metro area were closed for a total of two-and-a-half days due to somewhat hazardous traveling conditions on smaller roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of new snow reported at La Center and in Hockinson.

During an afternoon under a particularly cool and unstable airmass, small airmass thunderstorms developed over portions of the forecast area. At least one of these storms grew strong enough to produce hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported by Yale Reservoir Employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. The entire forecast area experienced heavy rainfall for an extended period of time. This abnormally long period of consistently heavy rainfall led to widespread flooding. Two rivers in southwest Washington exceeded major flood levels, which fueled extensive flood damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays River at Rosburg reached major flood levels, and one family was evacuated when flood waters inundated their home. County roads throughout the area received damage, and one family near the river lost an entire herd of cows. NOTE: Damage estimates are for the entire Wahkiakum county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. The entire forecast area experienced heavy rainfall for an extended period of time. This abnormally long period of consistently heavy rainfall led to widespread flooding. Two rivers in southwest Washington exceeded major flood levels, which fueled extensive flood damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willapa River flooded near Willapa, reaching major flood levels. Downtown Raymond had a depth of 2 feet of flood water due to a combination of coastal flooding and flooding of the Willapa. Residents in the Willapa valley stated they had never seen the Willapa river as high as it was. Also, minor flooding of the Naselle river sent some water over Hwy. 401. NOTE: Damage estimates are for the entire Pacific county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very powerful storms with origins in the Tropics brought hazardous weather to the Pacific Northwest. Each storm moved over the coastal Pacific waters in quick succession, and were unusually strong. With the stronger winds, seas built to heights of 44 to 48 feet. The seas combined with high tides caused widespread coastal flooding. Highest tides occurred Monday morning, December 3rd, and storm surges were between 2 and 5 feet over the predicted tide height. Also, as a result of the unusually high seas, both near shore buoys were lost in the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive areas along the Long Beach Peninsula were covered in ocean water. Flooding was worsened by the heavy rain and local river flooding. Wide portions of Hwy. 103 were completely under water. NOTE: Damage estimates are for the entire South Washington coast, namely the coastline of Pacific county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across portions of the Spokane area.  Snow accumulations include 5-6 on the south side of Spokane and in Cheney.  5 inches was also measured near Otis Orchards.

Ahead of a round of devastating storms in the Pacific Northwest, snow levels were very low, down to the valley floor in some regions. Precipitation fell as snow over the South Washington Cascades and foothills for an extended period of time before the much warmer systems moved in and changed all the precipitation to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen inches total accumulated snow fell in the higher reaches of the south Washington Cascade foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream produced heavy snow in the Cascades, while moist upslope flow produced heavy snow in the Blue Mountains. Snowfall in inches included 4 miles south southeast of Dixie (13), Ski Bluewood (12), 9 miles northwest of Roslyn (8), Roslyn (8).

Significant ice accumulations along with heavy snow fell along the East Slopes of the North Washington Cascades.  One inch of liquid precipitation, most of which was freezing rain, fell 5 miles NNW of Plain.  In Winthrop, approximately a quarter inch of ice accumulated.  Near Stevens Pass summit, 10 inches of snow fell.  In the Wenatchee area, light accumulations of freezing rain led to very icy roadways which delayed schools and contributed to a few traffic accidents.

A small but strong short wave brought heavy snow across portions of the Washington Palouse.  Temperature initially were above freezing resulting in initial melting on roads...and then compact snow over ice as temperatures dropped below freezing.  This contributed to 19 reported weather related traffic accidents.  Highway 195 between Pullman and Colfax was closed from early afternoon through around 6 PM.  Snow amounts from this event include 5-6 in Pullman, and 4 inches in Colfax.

A powerful Pacific frontal system with strong winds swept through western Washington beginning the night of January 5th, and continuing the morning of the 6th.  |In western Washington, areas between Olympia and Seattle were impacted the evening of Jan 5, by southerly winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts up to 50 mph just ahead of the main frontal system. Around midnight, the system moved inland shifting to winds from a westerly direction with speeds of 25 to 35 mph, and gusts to 50 mph, around the Strait of Juan de Fuca, and into Island and Snohomish counties early on Jan 6. Over 100,000 customers lost power in Snohomish County alone, before the winds eased later on the morning of the sixth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 5,000 people lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over portions of the Northeast Mountains.  6 of snow was measured 13 miles northeast of Colville, and 4 measured 7 miles west of Deer Park.
About 25,000 people lost power as a strong Pacific frontal system moved through western Washington.  Downed trees and power lines blocked a few roads. Landslides closed several roads.

A deep Pacific storm system offshore created strong south to north pressure differences along the coast for an extended period of time. The unique characteristic of the high wind was its duration--high wind lasted for about 18 hours on the coast.  At one point, about 73,000 customers were without power.  It took about a week to get power restored to everyone.  ||This storm also brought a variety of hazardous winter weather elsewhere in western Washington including lowland snow, heavy rainfall, flooding, landslides and avalanches in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 3000 people lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred on the Chehalis, Skokomish, and Elwha Rivers.  In addition to the record flooding, major flooding occurred on the Bogachiel River.  Flooding also occurred on the Nooksack, Skagit, Stillaguamish, SF Stillaguamish, Snohomish, Skykomish, Snoqualmie, Puyallup, Deschutes, Nisqually, Skookumchuck, Dungeness Rivers and Issaquah Creek. Significant and damaging urban and small stream flooding occurred in Snohomish, King, Lewis, Thurston, Mason, and Kitsap counties, as 3 to 8 inches of rain fell over the area with the exception of 10 to 20 inches in the Willapa Hills and southern Olympic mountain areas.  At least 130 people had to be rescued by helicopter. Two men died as a result of river flooding - one near Winlock in Lewis county and another along the Tahuya river in Mason county. A landslide hit a house and buried a man in his sleep near Hoodsport in Mason county.||These events were part of a significant deep Pacific storm system offshore the also initially generated lowland snow in western Washington and later created strong winds in primarily the coastal region and avalanches in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few homes had up to 3 inches of water in their basements. Flood waters from Swift Creek washed away part of Cedar Flat Road cutting off about 250 homes from vehicular traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred on the Chehalis, Skokomish, and Elwha Rivers.  In addition to the record flooding, major flooding occurred on the Bogachiel River.  Flooding also occurred on the Nooksack, Skagit, Stillaguamish, SF Stillaguamish, Snohomish, Skykomish, Snoqualmie, Puyallup, Deschutes, Nisqually, Skookumchuck, Dungeness Rivers and Issaquah Creek. Significant and damaging urban and small stream flooding occurred in Snohomish, King, Lewis, Thurston, Mason, and Kitsap counties, as 3 to 8 inches of rain fell over the area with the exception of 10 to 20 inches in the Willapa Hills and southern Olympic mountain areas.  At least 130 people had to be rescued by helicopter. Two men died as a result of river flooding - one near Winlock in Lewis county and another along the Tahuya river in Mason county. A landslide hit a house and buried a man in his sleep near Hoodsport in Mason county.||These events were part of a significant deep Pacific storm system offshore the also initially generated lowland snow in western Washington and later created strong winds in primarily the coastal region and avalanches in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private school in Clinton was flooded by over an inch of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific storm system offshore created strong south to north pressure differences along the coast for an extended period of time. The unique characteristic of the high wind was its duration--high wind lasted for about 18 hours on the coast.  At one point, about 73,000 customers were without power.  It took about a week to get power restored to everyone.  ||This storm also brought a variety of hazardous winter weather elsewhere in western Washington including lowland snow, heavy rainfall, flooding, landslides and avalanches in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three homes in Forks received severe damage from fallen trees.  Several road in and around Forks were closed as was the local schools. Two power utility linemen were injured by wind and debris during the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four apartment buildings were evacuated in North Seattle, where some basement apartments had 3 feet of water in them.  About 30 people were evacuated from apartments in Woodinville. Many roads were closed, including parts of SR 522, water over I-405 led to heavy traffic all day.  About 20 roads were damaged throughout the county.  Nearly 5 inches of rain fell at SeaTac airport.  Nathan Hale High School in north Seattle was closed for a week due to water damage from Thorton Creek for the first time ever.  A few homes and businesses had water damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chehalis river at Centralia reached a record flood stage of 74.78 feet.  An 81-year old man disappeared along a stream near Winlock. More than 450 homes were damaged outside of the cities of Chehalis and Centralia.  About 65 homes were destroyed, while just over 200 had major damage.  Over 1800 farm animals died.  A 20 mile stretch of Interstate-5 was closed for 3 days as parts of the interstate were under as much as 10 feet of water as well as the major north-south rail line.  In Centralia about 350 homes were damaged or destroyed.  About 1100 people used shelters.  Many schools were closed for a week.  Some communities had no fresh water.  More than 20 square blocks near downtown Centralia were under water.

Rainfall and increased snowmelt caused minor flooding along the Naches and Yakima Rivers in Yakima County March 12-14. Both rivers crested between 1/2 foot and 1 foot above flood stage.  The Yakima River also caused minor flooding northwest of Cle Elum in Kittitas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Naches River at Naches crested at 17.84 feet with flood stage at 17 feet. Yakima River at Parker crested at 10.87 feet with flood stage at 10 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elk Meadows subdivision near Interstate 90 northwest of Cle Elum had water 1 foot deep over roads on March 12-13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sudden hailstorm on Highway 195 at Hume Road intersection near Steptoe contributed to a collision after a vehicle hit a patch of slush and collided with a semi-truck.  Three indirect injuries resulted from the collision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong lee side trough east of the Cascades led to strong damaging gravity wave winds.  Where these gravity waves mixed down to the surface, extensive to catastrophic damages occurred.  These winds mixed down in the Wenatchee, Manson, south Lake Chelan area, Quincy, and the Royal slope area of Royal City.  Numerous trees were blown down, several of which fell on power lines, cars and homes.  Extensive roof damage occurred to several homes and businesses.  Chelan County was hit especially hard where commissioners declared a state of emergency.  A total of 400-500 trees fell onto power lines in Chelan County. In Quincy, numerous irrigation systems with a length of a quarter mile and weighing almost 20 tons were blown over.  Around 90 power poles were blown over in Western Grant County between George and Royal Slope.  Rocks the size of golf balls were driven up off the ground by the wind.  Interstate 90 just east of George was closed from 2:40 PM till 4:00 PM when a downed line ripped a radio antenna off one vehicle, broke out a passenger side windshield of another vehicle, with the debris from the windshield damaging another vehicle.  At Pangborn Memorial Airport in Wenatchee, a peak wind gust of 72 MPH was recorded.  Twenty aircraft that were tied down came loose, with 12 receiving minor damage.  In the Wenatchee heights area 3,800 fruit trees, mainly Cherry, were damaged by the winds.  In the Manson area, a narrow line of damage was observed from the Loop Avenue area across Lake Chelan to a resort with wind gusts up to 74 MPH.  20 indirect injuries resulted from the storm.

A prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain and snow melt led to flooding in the Oakesdale area, with basement flooding in Colfax.  Precipitation amounts of 1-2 was common. McCoy and Spring Creeks in and near Oakesdale rose rapidly out of its banks, with water reaching the foundations of 9 homes, flooding basements and crawl spaces.  Flooding of Pine Creek also shut down both lanes of State Route 27 south of Tekoa.  In Colfax, Clay Street was flooded as run-off filled storm drains with mud.  Several other streets were flooded as well.  The run-off also entered into the basements of some homes.  The North Fork of the Palouse River reached 14 feet, resulting in flooding of Lion's Club Park.  Property damage from this event was not known, but estimated to be at $25,000.

High winds occurred across portions of the Moses Lake area.  A wind gust of 68 MPH was recorded in Desert Aire, and 58 MPH at Priest Rapids Dam.

A modified arctic front moved slowly south from British Columbia through western Washington January 10th into the early hours of January 11th, bringing heavy snow to many lowland areas from the Puget Sound area northward.  Snow amounts varied from a few inches up to 11 inches in Whatcom county. The late afternoon commute on the 10th was very slow, some people ended up walking instead. Many school districts were closed on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 8 inches of snow fell with the highest amount reported just north of Tacoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in portions of the Spokane area and Washington Palouse.  Wind gusts from this event include 69 MPH at Otis Orchards, 58 MPH at Uniontown, 56 MPH in Pullman, 55 MPH at the Spokane Airport, and 45 MPH at Post Falls.  The wind gusts damaged power lines, leaving about 8,000 homes without power.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $2,000.

An unseasonably cold upper level low tracked across Central Washington, with convective moderate to heavy snow showers in the mountains. Mission Ridge Ski Resort received an estimated 10 inches of accumulation, with Badger Mountain picking up 6-8 inches of snow.  Snow was observed as low as 3,000 feet.  Ten power poles were snapped on Badger Mountain, knocking out power to 94 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm combined with sufficient onshore flow brought heavy snowfall to the southwest Washington Cascades. Smaller amounts fell over the Cascade foothills, and in higher elevations of the western Columbia Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amounts between 18 and 20 inches fell, based on similar conditions experienced in the north Oregon Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked through Asotin County, where quarter-sized hail was observed as well as downed power lines.

A strong short wave passing through eastern Washington contributed to a few severe thunderstorm reports.  An estimated 60 MPH wind gust occurred at Millwood in Spokane county, with an estimated gust of 58 MPH at Diamond Lake in Pend Oreille county.

A strong thunderstorm moving up from the southwest tracked across Whitman County, knocking out power to 4,000 customers in the west part of the county as well as Colfax.  West county areas that lost power included Diamond, Ewan, Ralston, and Marengo.  Two power posts were broken dear the Shawnee substation.

Up to 8 inches of snow was reported in the interior of western Washington.  ||This event was part of a suite of hazardous winter weather as a deep Pacific storm system offshore also later created strong damaging winds primarily in the coastal region, heavy rainfall, flooding, landslides and avalanches in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another batch of heavy snow fell.  Average amount at least 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful pacific storm brought very strong winds to portions of southwest Washington.  Many locations along the coast saw wind gusts in excess of 80 mph, while areas inland saw less severe, however still damaging, winds.  Power outages were sustained in multiple communities across the area. At it's peak, approximately 17000 Washington customers were without power due to this storm. Many power outages were due to downed trees or tree limbs knocking down power lines.  This also caused multiple highways from the coast to the interior to be closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were reported at the Toke Point NOS tide gauge, and recorded sustained winds of 50 kt. Other wind reports include: gusts to 61 kt at Bay Center; gusts to 60 kt at Oysterville.

Ahead of a round of devastating storms in the Pacific Northwest, snow levels were very low, down to the valley floor in some regions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two hikers were killed, and one injured, by an avalanche near Snoqualmie Pass. 3 snowboarders are presumed dead near Crystal Mountain.||The snowfall that led to the avalanches was part of a deep Pacific storm system offshore the also created lowland snow in western Washington, strong winds particularly in the coastal region as well as heavy rainfall, flooding and landslides elsewhere in western Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowboarders were presumed dead near Crystal Mountain.  They were found in mid June 2008 in their sleeping bags once the snow had melted enough.  It is presumed they were buried in an avalanche during the night of Dec 1 or early morning of Dec 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked through Spokane County.  Several 12-14 inch trees were sheared off in Rockford, landing on roofs.

Yet another in a series of cold, moist Pacific frontal systems brought heavy snow to the Cascades. Westerly flow behind the advancing cold front orographically enhanced snowfall potential for this storm. This storm was the fourth storm in six days to bring heavy snow to the Cascades, continuing to build the steadily increasing mountain snow pack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 20 inches total accumulated snowfall was reported by many SNOTEL reporting sites surrounding Mt. St. Helens, including Swift Creek, Sheep Canyon, June Lake, Lone Pine, Spirit Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a round of devastating storms in the Pacific Northwest, snow levels were very low, down to the valley floor in some regions. Precipitation fell as snow over the South Washington Cascades and foothills for an extended period of time before the much warmer systems moved in and changed all the precipitation to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 15 and 30 inches total accumulated snow fell, based on reports from multiple SNOTEL sensors surrounding Mt. St. Helens, including June Lake, Swift Creek, Sheep Canyon, and Spencer Meadows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm and associated cold front brought strong winds to the coast and heavy snow to the Cascades. Behind the front in the unstable airmass, snow levels dropped and cold air hung over the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 20 and 30 inches total snowfall accumulation was reported by many SNOTEL stations on Mt. St. Helens, including Sheep Canyon, Swift Creek, Lone Pine, and Spencer Meadows.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 17 and 27 inches total accumulated snowfall were reported by many SNOTEL sites surrounding Mt. St. Helens including Spencer Meadows, Sheep Canyon, Swift Creek, and June Lake.

The Easy Street Fire, centered four miles northwest of Wenatchee, was a human caused fire that burned 5,209 acres.  One outbuilding was lost in the fire.

A boogie boarder recreating in the the surf zone near Long Beach, WA drown after he was pulled out to sea by a rip tide. The Coast Guard and local Search and Rescue folks were called after the boy was reported missing, but was unable to locate him. The body was discovered the next morning near the Bolstad Approach, nearly two miles from where he was last seen.
The Tunk Grade Fire, centered 11 miles north of Omak, was a lightning caused fire that burned 15,540 acres.  Ten outbuildings and one structure were lost in the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very strong storms brought hazardous winter weather conditions to the Pacific northwest. The first of these storms rocked the coast of south Washington with very strong winds. The storm approached from the west-southwest overnight Saturday night, December 1st, and the frontal system approached the coastline by early Sunday, December 2nd. The strongest winds were experienced Sunday afternoon and early evening. The high wind speeds associated with this storm brought down not only tree limbs, but whole trees. Multiple roads were closed along the coast and up into the Willapa Hills due to downed trees and power lines.  A woman, unknown age from Seaview, WA died when her house burned down while burning candles after an extended power outage due to high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded at Cape Disappointment. Other impressive wind reports from this event include: gusts to 85 kt on Klipsan Beach, located south of Ocean Park on the Long Beach Peninsula; gusts to 65 kt at Toke Point, near Tokeland on the Willapa Bay; gusts to 63 in Bay Center; gusts to 58 kt in Oysterville, on the Long Beach Peninsula; gusts to 52 kt in Long Beach.|Also, just south of Long Beach in Seaview, an elderly woman died in her home after the house burned down. The house fire was caused by candles the woman had lit after strong winds knocked out power to her home.|Other Wind gusts were also recorded on Dec 2 on Toke Point, near Tokeland on the Willapa Bay. Wind gusts of 54 kt were also recorded in Bay Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wautoma Fire, 12 miles north of Benton City, started August 16 and burned 67,000 acres by August 18 when it was brought under control.  It was burned dry grass and sage and was fanned by strong winds.
The Marshall Group Fire, centered four miles northeast of Cheney, was an equipment caused fire that burned 365 acres.  One structure and one outbuilding was lost in the fire.
The Cottonwood Creek Fire, centered six miles ESE of Peoly, burned 3,578 acres.
